summary: Yegge responds to accusations of being a snobbish interviewer by drawing satirical parallels to other demanding professions like airline pilots and brain surgeons. He argues that top tech companies have legitimately high standards because they've been burned by lowering them, and that software engineering has fragmented into specialized roles just like medicine. The post acknowledges interviewing is imperfect but defends the practice of testing candidates on computer science fundamentals, while advising candidates to research companies, vote with their feet, and improve the process from within rather than complaining anonymously online.
key_points: Tech companies test on algorithms, data structures, and CS fundamentals because engineers actually use these skills daily - lowering standards leads to costly failures
The software industry has fragmented into specializations; being good at web development doesn't qualify you for kernel work and vice versa
Interviewing is imperfect and somewhat of a 'crapshoot' but companies all do it roughly the same way
Motivation and desire to improve oneself matters more than current knowledge - candidates on an upward curve get more leeway
Big company jobs aren't as glamorous as they seem; startups offer more excitement but more risk
money_quotes: If you want a job at a company like Microsoft, Yahoo!, Apple, or Amazon.com, they're going to have high standards. It doesn't matter if you 'know how to program'.
But when they lower their standards, they get burned. The 747 crashes, the patient dies, the juggler drops a bowling pin on someone's head, the tiger rips someone's throat out.

Putting together a pretty Ruby on Rails site is no small feat. Learning to program is no small feat. Many people try and fail to get even that far. But it's 3- to 5-ball juggling, and it just doesn't cut it for the Cirque du Soleils and private hospitals of the software industry.
Our industry has fragmented, just the way the medical industry has, and there are different job roles now.
I count motivation (that is, the desire to improve oneself) higher than any other factor in an interview.
The truth about interviewing is that everyone does it their own way, and it's always about the same: people interview for what they know.
If you don't know something, don't go berserk on us. Just go read about it on Wikipedia. It's not as hard as you think!
key_insight: Technical interviewing's high standards exist because software engineering is a demanding profession with real consequences for failure, and rather than complaining about it, candidates should either acquire the skills companies need or find companies whose priorities match their own.
